question_text: Richard Willstatter (1915 Chemistry Nobel Laureate) treated diamine A with excess methyl iodide, and then with excess Ag2O and heat, whereupon a hydrocarbon B, C8H8, distilled from the reaction mixture. Compound B rapidly reacted with excess Br2 under mild conditions. Treatment of compound C in the same way gave a hydrocarbon D, C6H6, which did not react with excess Br2. Identify compounds B, D, and E and in a sentence or two explain the different reactivity of B and D towards Br2 (Willstatter concluded from these experiments that compound D could not be an alkene). The images show diamine A and C, both with N(CH3)2 substituents; B is a cyclooctatetraene; D is benzene; and E is a perbrominated cyclooctane derivative formed by bromine addition to B. Also include the bonus question: What is the name of the reaction that converts A into B and C into D?
canonical_answer: B = cyclooctatetraene; D = benzene; E = perbromocyclooctane; Compound D (benzene) is very stable because it is aromatic and therefore does not react with Br2. Compound B is not aromatic and consequently is less stable and more prone to reacting with Br2.; Bonus: Hofmann Elimination

question_text: Outline a sequence of reactions that would bring about the conversion of aniline into benzylamine. The scheme shows aniline converted to a benzonitrile intermediate using NaNO2/HCl followed by CuCN, then reduction of benzonitrile to benzylamine using LiAlH4 followed by OH-/H2O work-up. Hydrogen and catalyst were also accepted for the reduction step.

Arrange the amines within each set in order of increasing basicity in aqueous solution, least basic first. Indicate your answers by placing the compound numbers in the boxes for each series. (a) Compounds: 1) H2NCH2CH2COMe (methyl ester), 2) ethylamine (NH2CH2CH3), 3) protonated ethylenediamine (H3N+CH2CH2NH2). Place answer order for least basic to most basic. (b) Compounds: 1) benzylamine (benzene-CH2-NH2), 2) 4-nitrobenzylamine (benzene-ring with NO2 para to CH2NH2), 3) cyclohexylamine (cyclohexane-NH2), 4) aniline (benzene-NH2). Arrange these four amines in order of increasing basicity in aqueous solution, least basic first, most basic last.

You are asked to use an extractive procedure to separate p-methoxyacetanilide (1) from p-methoxyaniline (2). Devise a dichloromethane-aqueous protocol by selecting the appropriate aqueous solution (A, B, C, or D: A = 5% NaOH pH 14; B = 5% HCl pH -0.8; C = 5% NaHCO3 pH 8.0; D = 25% NaCl pH 7.2) and then identify the location of compounds 1 and 2 in the biphasic system within the separatory funnel (organic layer = CH2Cl2 at bottom; aqueous layer at top, marked B). Mark your answers in the boxes for compounds 1 and 2 and aqueous phase letter B. Bonus: Once you have separated 1 from 2, how would you retrieve the compound in the aqueous layer? Treat the aqueous layer with base, then extract with organic solvent and collect the organic layer.
canonical_answer: aqueous solution = B; compound 1 = organic layer; compound 2 = aqueous layer; aqueous layer letter = B; Bonus = Treat the aqueous layer with base, then extract with organic solvent and collect the organic layer

question_text: The antidepressant drug tranylcypromine is a primary amine with the amino group on a cyclopropane ring. Show how you would convert an enantiomer of 2-phenylcyclopropanecarboxylic acid (which is made from trans-cinnamic acid) to tranylcypromine. (Hint: If someone is asking you to rearrange your atoms, it is best to be courteous.) Bonus: What reaction would you use to effect the first transformation shown below? (The reaction scheme shows trans-cinnamic acid converted to (1S,2S)-2-phenylcyclopropanecarboxylic acid, then to an acid chloride using SOCl2, then to an azide with NaN3, then by Curtius Rearrangement with heat and H2O to an amine, and separately an alternative conversion of the acid chloride to tranylcypromine via NH3 then Br2, NaOH, H2O by Hofmann Rearrangement.)
canonical_answer: Simmons–Smith reaction; Conversion sequence: trans-cinnamic acid → (1S,2S)-2-phenylcyclopropanecarboxylic acid → acid chloride (SOCl2) → acyl azide (NaN3) → amine (Curtius rearrangement) or acid chloride → primary amine (NH3, then Hofmann rearrangement with Br2, NaOH, H2O)

question_text: Alizarin yellow R is an azo dye that changes color from yellow to red between pH 10.2 and 12.2. Outline a synthesis of alizarin yellow R from aniline, salicylic acid (o-hydroxybenzoic acid), and any other reagents. (The reaction scheme shows: aniline acetylated with acetyl chloride and pyridine to acetanilide; nitration of acetanilide with HNO3/H2SO4; hydrolysis with NaOH/H2O to 2-nitroaniline; diazotization of 2-nitroaniline with NaNO2/HCl to diazonium salt; coupling with salicylic acid to form alizarin yellow R.)
canonical_answer: Amination of aniline with acetyl chloride and pyridine to give acetanilide; nitration of acetanilide with HNO3 and H2SO4 to nitroacetanilide; hydrolysis of nitroacetanilide with NaOH and H2O to 2-nitroaniline; diazotization of 2-nitroaniline with NaNO2 and HCl to form diazonium salt; azo coupling of diazonium salt with salicylic acid yield alizarin yellow R
